heroOverview: A mandibular fracture is a break in the lower jaw — the only mobile bone of the face — and accounts for roughly 36-70% of all facial fractures in most trauma series. The mandible forms a U-shaped ring with the skull at the two temporomandibular joints, and like any ring bone it tends to break in two places simultaneously. Road traffic collisions and interpersonal violence are the two leading causes in adults, together accounting for over 70% of cases in published series. Diagnosis requires clinical examination combined with CT imaging, which has largely replaced plain dental X-rays for initial workup. Treatment is tailored to the fracture site and displacement: condylar and subcondylar fractures are often managed non-operatively with functional therapy, while displaced body and symphysis fractures are treated with open reduction and internal fixation using titanium miniplates. With modern surgical care, the large majority of patients recover full jaw function and occlusion within 6-12 weeks.

keyStats.prevalence: Mandibular fractures account for 36-70% of all facial fractures in trauma studies; incidence estimated at 11-40 per 100,000 population annually in high-income countries
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence in males aged 18-35, who account for 70-80% of cases; aetiology skews toward assault in younger adults and motor vehicle collision in older adults
keyStats.avgAge: Most common in young adult men aged 20-30; a second, smaller peak exists in the elderly from low-energy falls
keyStats.globalCases: Mandibular fractures are among the most common maxillofacial injuries worldwide; exact global incidence is not reliably tracked, but millions of cases are treated annually

definition: A mandibular fracture (ICD-10: S02.6 — Fracture of mandible) is a disruption of continuity of the mandibular cortical or cancellous bone, ranging from a greenstick or incomplete crack to a comminuted or compound fracture involving the overlying skin or oral mucosa. The mandible is a single horseshoe-shaped bone articulating bilaterally at the temporomandibular joint (TMJ) via the condylar heads; its regions — condyle, subcondyle, ramus, angle, body, parasymphysis, and symphysis — each have distinct treatment implications because of the differing muscular forces and bite loads applied to each segment. The fracture is termed compound (open) when it communicates with the oral cavity or external skin, which includes most body and symphysis fractures given the proximity of dental roots and the alveolar mucosa. Classified by location, displacement, and favourability (whether muscle pull tends to reduce or displace the fracture), mandibular fractures fall under the scope of oral and maxillofacial surgery (OMFS). Approximately 50% of mandibular fractures occur at the condyle or subcondylar region; the body accounts for about 25-30%, the angle for 20-25%, and the symphysis or parasymphysis for 15-20%. Associated injuries — dental fractures, soft tissue lacerations, alveolar bone damage, and cervical spine injury — must be actively sought because they influence both immediate management and the rehabilitation plan.

typesClassification.description: The most common site (approximately 30-50% of mandibular fractures); the condyle is the rounded head that articulates with the skull base. Intracapsular fractures involve the condyle within the joint capsule; subcondylar fractures occur just below the capsule. Many are managed non-operatively with physiotherapy in adults and almost always in children to preserve growth potential.

Fracture through the mandibular angle, the region at the junction of the body and ramus, where the third molar (wisdom tooth) is often present. The angle is a zone of relative weakness; impacted third molars increase the risk of a fracture at this site and may influence whether the tooth is retained or extracted during repair.

Fracture through the tooth-bearing portion of the mandibular body between the canine and third molar region. Usually compound because it communicates with the oral mucosa via the dental alveolus. Frequently treated with open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) using miniplates.

Fractures at the chin midline (symphysis) or just to either side (parasymphysis), often from a direct blow. These fractures splay the anterior mandible and impair anterior tooth alignment; they are almost always treated with ORIF.

Fracture through the vertical ramus, accounting for roughly 3-9% of cases; often undisplaced due to the overlying masseter and pterygoid muscles which act as a splint. Usually managed conservatively unless severely displaced.

Multiple bone fragments, typically resulting from high-energy injury such as a gunshot wound or high-speed road collision; requires more complex reconstruction, sometimes with external fixation or bone grafting.

primarySymptoms: Malocclusion — the upper and lower teeth no longer meet properly when biting, the most reliable clinical indicator of a mandibular fracture in a conscious cooperative patient.
Pain localised to the fracture site, which worsens markedly with jaw movement, eating, or attempted mouth opening.
Trismus — limited mouth opening below 35 mm, caused by pain, haematoma, muscle spasm, or mechanical displacement at the fracture site.
Swelling and visible deformity over the lower jaw, chin, or angle of the jaw, typically appearing within hours of injury.
Paraesthesia or numbness of the lower lip and chin on the affected side, from injury to the inferior alveolar nerve as it runs through the mandibular canal.
Step deformity felt along the lower teeth when the tongue is run across the dental arch, indicating displaced bony segments.
Bruising and ecchymosis in the floor of the mouth (sublingual haematoma), a highly specific sign of mandibular fracture when present.
Tooth looseness, avulsion, or fracture at or adjacent to the fracture line, which may be confused with isolated dental trauma if the bony injury is not sought.

Airway distress from bilateral body or symphysis fractures in which the anterior tongue loses its muscular attachment and falls back into the oropharynx — a rare but potentially fatal complication requiring immediate airway management.
Deviation of the mandible on opening toward the side of a displaced subcondylar fracture, reflecting unopposed pull of the contralateral pterygoid muscles.
earlyWarningSigns: A clicking or grinding sensation when biting after a blow to the chin or jaw.
Mild tenderness over the TMJ or lower jaw angle after a fall or assault, even without swelling.
A single tooth that suddenly feels higher than its neighbours after facial trauma.
Difficulty speaking clearly or chewing soft food after a facial injury.
emergencySigns: Airway obstruction — gurgling breathing, inability to lie flat, or visible swelling of the floor of the mouth indicating haematoma that is displacing the tongue; requires immediate airway intervention.
Profuse bleeding from the mouth or lips that does not respond to direct pressure, suggesting arterial injury within the fracture site.
Loss of consciousness, confusion, or seizure after the facial injury, which may indicate concurrent traumatic brain injury requiring urgent CT head.
Signs of haemorrhagic shock — pallor, rapid weak pulse, low blood pressure — suggesting associated vascular injury, especially in comminuted or penetrating mandibular fractures.

causes.description: High-energy frontal or lateral impact to the face, either from the steering wheel, airbag, or window, applies forces that typically fracture the symphysis anteriorly and the contralateral condyle simultaneously due to the transmission of force through the mandibular arch.

A direct blow to the jaw — the most common cause in young urban males — typically fractures the angle or body at the point of impact, with a contrecoup condylar fracture on the opposite side from the blow in approximately 30-40% of cases.

Ground-level falls are the predominant cause in older adults, in whom age-related bone density reduction makes the mandible more susceptible to fracture from lower-energy impact; falls onto the chin from standing height can produce bilateral condylar fractures.

Contact sports — boxing, football, rugby, ice hockey — account for a significant proportion of mandibular fractures; mouthguard use reduces fracture risk by approximately 50-60% in studies of contact athletes.

Mandibular fractures can occur through weakened bone in the context of osteonecrosis of the jaw (medication-related, from bisphosphonates or denosumab), primary or metastatic malignancy involving the mandible, or severe periodontitis that has caused significant bone loss around the tooth roots.

riskFactors.description: Young men are 3-4 times more likely to sustain a mandibular fracture than women of the same age, primarily driven by higher rates of interpersonal violence, motor vehicle risk-taking, and contact sports participation.

Alcohol intoxication is documented in 30-50% of assault-related facial fractures; it both increases the risk of being involved in violence and impairs protective reflexes that might otherwise minimise impact force.

Unrestrained vehicle occupants sustain maxillofacial injuries including mandibular fractures at approximately 3-fold higher rates than belted occupants; airbags without seatbelts still carry significant facial injury risk.
Mouthguard use reduces jaw fracture risk in contact athletes by absorbing and distributing impact energy; less than 50% of recreational contact sport participants wear mouthguards consistently.

Bisphosphonate and denosumab therapy for osteoporosis or bone metastases can cause osteonecrosis of the jaw, substantially weakening the mandible and predisposing to spontaneous or low-energy fracture.

Impacted lower wisdom teeth, large mandibular cysts, or radiolucent lesions at the mandibular angle create stress risers that make that region significantly more vulnerable to fracture from ordinary forces.

Reduced cortical bone density in the mandible, associated with systemic osteoporosis and tooth loss (which causes alveolar bone resorption), lowers the energy required to fracture the jaw in elderly individuals.

affectedDemographics: Young adult men aged 18-35, the highest-incidence group worldwide
Individuals involved in contact sports without appropriate protective gear
People living in areas with high rates of interpersonal violence
Patients with medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw on bisphosphonates or denosumab
Children (predominantly condylar fractures from playground falls, often managed without surgery)
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of a mandibular fracture begins with a systematic clinical examination: the clinician inspects for facial asymmetry, swelling, laceration, and step deformities; palpates along the lower border of the mandible for a gap, tenderness, or crepitus; evaluates occlusion by asking the patient to bite; and tests lower lip and chin sensation. Malocclusion is the most sensitive clinical indicator and should be actively sought rather than assumed absent. The standard imaging protocol has shifted to fine-cut maxillofacial CT (0.625 mm slices with sagittal and coronal reconstructions and 3D rendering), which visualises all mandibular regions including the condyles with superior accuracy compared with plain radiographs. Panoramic dental X-ray (OPG or pantomogram) remains a useful adjunct or alternative where CT is not immediately available, providing a sweeping view of the entire mandibular arch in a single image. Plain films are largely obsolete for primary diagnosis. Condylar fractures are particularly underdiagnosed on plain films; dedicated CT ensures they are not missed. Associated injuries — cervical spine injury (in up to 4-8% of mandibular fractures from high-energy mechanisms), intracranial injury, and dental avulsions — must be evaluated in parallel. All dental fragments must be accounted for on imaging to exclude aspiration. Blood tests are not diagnostic but a full blood count and group-and-save are taken before any planned surgical repair.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard imaging for mandibular fractures; provides multiplanar and 3D reconstructions that reveal fracture site, displacement, condylar head position, and occlusal alignment better than any other modality.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in the CT scanner for 5-10 minutes; no contrast is required. Fine sections through the face are reconstructed into 2D and 3D images for the surgeon to plan treatment. The scan is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A sweeping dental X-ray capturing the entire mandibular arch from condyle to condyle in a single image; useful for confirming fracture location and assessing the dental roots at the fracture site.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You stand or sit at a machine that rotates an X-ray arm around your head in about 15 seconds; you bite on a small plastic peg to hold still. No injection is required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Clinical evaluation of how the upper and lower teeth meet; malocclusion — teeth not aligning normally — is the most sensitive bedside indicator of an unstable mandibular fracture.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician asks you to bite your back teeth together gently and observes and photographs your bite from the front and sides. They also feel along the lower jaw for tenderness and steps.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes a baseline for lower lip and chin sensation before surgery; allows post-operative monitoring of recovery of the inferior alveolar nerve, which passes through the mandibular canal and is frequently neuropraxic in body and angle fractures.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician uses a fine probe to test sensation on your lower lip and chin, comparing the injured and uninjured sides. This takes less than two minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for concurrent cervical spine injury, which coexists in 4-8% of high-energy mandibular fractures; a missed C-spine injury carries serious neurological risk.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Additional views of the neck are taken as part of the same CT session or as separate plain films; you must remain still and avoid flexing the neck until cervical injury is excluded.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes dental fragment aspiration into the bronchial tree, which can cause an aspiration pneumonia if a small tooth or bone chip is inhaled during the injury or examination.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard upright chest X-ray lasting seconds; the radiologist checks the airway and bronchi for any opaque foreign body.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of mandibular fractures is guided by four principles: re-establishing stable dental occlusion, maintaining or restoring the functional mandibular arc, ensuring adequate airway, and preventing infection in compound fractures. Non-operative management — closed treatment with intermaxillary fixation (IMF, or wiring the jaws together) or functional therapy alone — is appropriate for undisplaced fractures, condylar fractures in most adults, and greenstick fractures in children. IMF holds the teeth in occlusion for 3-6 weeks while bone heals; modern IMF uses arch bars or screw-and-wire systems and allows the patient to eat liquidised foods. Open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) with titanium miniplates and screws is the standard for displaced body, angle, and symphysis fractures; it restores anatomical bony alignment under direct vision and usually allows early mobilisation without prolonged jaw wiring. The Champy technique places miniplates along the mandibular tension band (the upper outer surface of the mandible) and relies on natural functional loading to achieve stability. Condylar fractures involving dislocation of the condylar head out of the glenoid fossa, bilateral condylar fractures, or those associated with a foreign body may be treated operatively via a retromandibular or transparotid approach. Antibiotic prophylaxis (typically amoxicillin or co-amoxiclav for 5-7 days) is routine for all compound fractures entering the oral cavity. Dental hygiene with chlorhexidine mouth rinse is essential because the oral flora represents the primary source of post-operative infection. Physiotherapy and mouth-opening exercises begin once the fixation is stable, targeting restoration of normal mouth opening within 8-12 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Intermaxillary fixation (IMF / jaw wiring)
medicalTreatments.description: Arch bars, MMF screws, or hybrid wiring systems are applied to the upper and lower teeth to hold the jaws in correct occlusal relationship while the fracture heals. Used as definitive treatment for undisplaced or condylar fractures and as an adjunct during ORIF to maintain occlusion while plates are applied.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Occlusal outcomes equivalent to ORIF in undisplaced fractures; reported success rate of approximately 90-95% for condylar fractures managed non-operatively in adults. Requires 3-6 weeks of restricted diet.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotic prophylaxis (amoxicillin-clavulanate or clindamycin)
medicalTreatments.description: All compound mandibular fractures communicate with the oral cavity and its heavy bacterial load; 5-7 days of prophylactic antibiotics covering oral streptococci and anaerobes reduces post-operative infection rates from approximately 15-25% to 5-10%.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses show prophylactic antibiotics reduce wound infection rates by approximately 50-60% in mandibular ORIF; routine practice in all major trauma guidelines.

medicalTreatments.name: Chlorhexidine 0.12-0.2% mouth rinse
medicalTreatments.description: Twice-daily chlorhexidine rinse throughout the healing period reduces intraoral bacterial load at the fracture site and plate hardware; used as an adjunct to mechanical oral hygiene when jaw wiring limits normal toothbrushing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces plaque scores by 50-60% and contributes to the low surgical site infection rates seen in well-managed mandibular fracture series.

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesia (NSAIDs and paracetamol)
medicalTreatments.description: Multimodal analgesia combining regular paracetamol (1 g four times daily) with ibuprofen or diclofenac provides effective post-operative pain control with opioid-sparing; opioids are reserved for the first 24-48 hours after ORIF when pain is most severe.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combination NSAID and paracetamol achieves pain scores 30-40% lower than either agent alone in post-maxillofacial surgery studies.

surgicalOptions.name: Open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) with miniplates
surgicalOptions.description: The standard operative approach for displaced body, angle, and symphysis fractures; the fracture is exposed through an intraoral incision (or rarely an external neck incision for the angle), reduced under direct vision to restore occlusion, and held with 2.0 mm titanium miniplates and mono- or bicortical screws along the Champy lines of tension.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Malocclusion-free outcome in over 92-95% of cases in experienced units; infection rate below 5-10% with antibiotic prophylaxis; plate removal required in approximately 5-10% due to infection, plate exposure, or patient request.

surgicalOptions.name: Open reduction with retromandibular or transparotid approach (condylar ORIF)
surgicalOptions.description: Operative reduction and plating of displaced or dislocated condylar fractures via a small incision behind or through the parotid gland, restoring condylar height and anatomical position when non-operative management is predicted to yield an inadequate result.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Condylar ORIF achieves anatomical reduction in approximately 90-95% of cases; patients regain mouth opening above 40 mm in over 85% of cases with post-operative physiotherapy.

surgicalOptions.name: External fixation
surgicalOptions.description: Pins inserted into the mandible external to the skin connected by a bridging rod; reserved for severely comminuted fractures with significant soft tissue loss, infected fractures, or reconstruction-stage cases where immediate ORIF is not feasible.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Provides adequate temporary stabilisation in complex trauma; ultimate functional outcomes depend on subsequent definitive reconstruction.

surgicalOptions.name: Reconstruction plate fixation
surgicalOptions.description: Heavy-gauge 2.4 mm load-bearing titanium reconstruction plates used for severely comminuted fractures, fractures through pathological bone, or as part of segmental mandibular reconstruction after tumour resection. May be combined with bone graft.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Adequate stability in over 90% of comminuted cases; implant failure or infection necessitating plate removal occurs in approximately 8-15% of reconstruction plate cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Functional therapy and physiotherapy (condylar fractures)
alternativeTreatments.description: For condylar and subcondylar fractures — particularly in children and adolescents — early active mobilisation with jaw physiotherapy exercises and an occlusal splint is a well-established non-surgical alternative; it preserves the condylar growth centre in children and yields equivalent functional outcomes to surgery in many adult condylar fracture types.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional support with liquidised diet
alternativeTreatments.description: During intermaxillary fixation, caloric intake must be maintained through liquidised foods and nutritional supplements delivered through the interdental gaps or via a nasogastric tube in severe cases; dietitian input optimises healing.

whySeeSpecialist: Any suspected mandibular fracture should be assessed by an oral and maxillofacial surgeon, ideally within 24-72 hours of injury. The correct fracture classification drives the treatment choice — non-operative versus ORIF — and incorrect management leads to malunion, malocclusion, temporomandibular joint arthritis, and chronic pain. Specialist input is also essential for managing associated dental injuries, preserving the inferior alveolar nerve, and planning safe antibiotic prophylaxis.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an oral and maxillofacial surgeon with specific experience in facial trauma, ideally working within a designated trauma unit. For bilateral condylar fractures or complex comminuted injuries, a centre with access to a CT scanner, dental laboratory support for occlusal assessment, and an anaesthetic team experienced in shared-airway maxillofacial surgery is important. Post-operative physiotherapy from a therapist experienced in TMJ rehabilitation substantially improves mouth-opening outcomes.
preventionStrategies: Wear a properly fitted mouthguard for all contact and collision sports — rugby, boxing, football, ice hockey, basketball; mouthguards reduce jaw fracture risk by approximately 50-60%.
Always wear a seatbelt in motor vehicles; it reduces facial injury severity even when an airbag deploys.
Wear a full-face helmet for motorcycling and cycling rather than an open-face helmet.
Avoid high-risk situations for interpersonal violence and moderate alcohol consumption, which impairs protective responses.
Inform your dentist and oral surgeon if you take bisphosphonates or denosumab before any invasive dental procedure, to enable appropriate drug holiday planning and reduce MRONJ risk.
Treat mandibular cysts and large odontogenic lesions promptly to prevent pathological fractures through weakened bone.
lifestyleModifications: Follow a soft or liquid diet for the duration advised by your surgeon — typically 6 weeks for IMF cases, 2-3 weeks after ORIF.
Perform prescribed jaw physiotherapy exercises religiously from the date your surgeon authorises them; inadequate exercise leads to reduced mouth opening that can become permanent.
Maintain meticulous oral hygiene with the tools provided (interdental brushes, chlorhexidine rinse, syringe irrigation) to protect the plate hardware from infection.
Avoid contact sports until your surgeon confirms radiological union and removes any restrictions — usually at 8-12 weeks.
Attend all follow-up appointments; plate hardware failure, implant infection, or developing malocclusion are easiest to address early.
Do not smoke during healing; smoking significantly impairs bone healing, doubles the infection risk in ORIF cases, and delays return to normal function.

recommended: Liquidised complete nutritional supplements (Fortisip, Ensure, Complan) to maintain caloric intake during wiring or early healing
Pureed vegetables, soups, yoghurt, soft scrambled egg, and mashed potato once limited mouth opening permits
Protein-rich foods (liquidised chicken, fish, legume soups) to support bone and soft tissue repair
Calcium-rich foods — dairy products or calcium-fortified plant milks — to support mineralisation of the healing fracture
Adequate hydration; dehydration worsens mucosal condition and increases infection risk in the oral cavity
avoid: All hard, crunchy, or chewy foods — bread crusts, raw vegetables, nuts, steak — until surgical clearance
Foods requiring bilateral molar contact, including chewing gum or toffees, for at least 6 weeks
Alcohol during healing, which impairs bone metabolism and increases bleeding risk
Hot drinks when the lower lip is numb from nerve injury, as thermal burns may not be detected

exerciseGuidelines: General physical activity can usually be resumed 2-4 weeks after ORIF as pain allows. Jaw-specific physiotherapy exercises — gentle progressive mouth opening against resistance, lateral excursion movements, protrusion exercises — should begin as soon as the surgeon authorises them, typically at 2-3 weeks for ORIF cases and immediately post-IMF removal. Achieving mouth opening above 40 mm by 8-12 weeks is the target; failure to progress warrants assessment for scar contracture or condylar ankylosis. No contact sports or activities with risk of facial re-injury until confirmed radiological healing, usually at 8-12 weeks.
dailyManagement: Clean the hardware and oral cavity at least three times daily with the recommended regimen; post-meal irrigation with a syringe removes food debris from around arch bars and plates.
Monitor the fracture repair for signs of infection: increasing pain after post-operative day 3, fever above 38°C, purulent discharge from intraoral wounds, or plate exposure.
Perform jaw opening exercises at the prescribed frequency — typically 10-15 repetitions, three to four times daily — from the date your surgeon advises.

Carry wire cutters or scissors (for IMF patients) at all times in case of vomiting, which can cause aspiration; your surgeon will advise which fixation screws or ties to remove to release the jaws quickly.
Seek same-day review if you experience difficulty breathing, increasing swelling, fever, or the sensation that the bite has shifted.
prognosis: The prognosis for mandibular fractures managed appropriately by an experienced maxillofacial surgeon is very good. More than 90% of patients achieve stable occlusion and normal jaw function within 8-12 weeks. Inferior alveolar nerve neuropraxia — producing lower lip numbness — recovers fully in approximately 70-80% of patients over 3-6 months; persistent numbness beyond 12 months reflects more severe nerve injury and may be permanent in a minority. Condylar fractures in children carry an excellent prognosis with non-operative management and have a notable capacity for remodelling due to the condyle's growth potential. Delayed or inappropriate treatment is the primary cause of long-term complications, including malocclusion requiring corrective osteotomy, condylar resorption, or TMJ ankylosis.
recoveryTimeline: For ORIF cases, most patients are discharged 1-3 days after surgery, take liquid or soft foods for 2-3 weeks, and return to a soft diet by 4-6 weeks; physiotherapy starts at 2-3 weeks and targets normal opening by 8-12 weeks. For IMF (jaw wiring) without surgery, the jaws are kept wired for 3-6 weeks; once released, physiotherapy is essential to regain opening. Return to work in sedentary roles typically occurs at 2-3 weeks; manual or physically demanding work requires 6-8 weeks. Driving is generally restricted until normal bite and jaw control are restored.
complications: Malocclusion — teeth not meeting correctly after healing; the most common functional complication of inadequately reduced or malunited fractures, potentially requiring corrective osteotomy.
Infection and plate hardware failure — post-operative wound infection in approximately 5-10% of ORIF cases; infected hardware typically requires plate removal after union has occurred.
Inferior alveolar nerve injury — persistent lower lip and chin numbness; full recovery in approximately 70-80% of cases within 6-12 months; permanent neuropathy in a minority.

Temporomandibular joint ankylosis — fibrous or bony fusion of the joint after intracapsular condylar fracture, particularly in children; causes severe limitation of mouth opening and requires surgical release if established.

Non-union or malunion — failure of the fracture to heal (non-union) or healing in an incorrect position (malunion); more common with inadequate fixation, infection, or failure to comply with dietary restrictions.
Orofacial contracture and reduced mouth opening — scarring of jaw musculature or articular adhesions after prolonged IMF or complex injury; requires intensive physiotherapy or surgical release.

supportResources.name: British Association of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeons — Jaw Fracture Information
supportResources.description: UK patient information from the specialist surgical society on mandibular fracture management, what to expect during treatment, and recovery guidance.

supportResources.name: American Association of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeons
supportResources.description: US specialist association providing patient-facing information on facial trauma, surgical procedures, and post-operative care.

supportResources.name: NHS — Broken Jaw (Mandibular Fracture)
supportResources.description: NHS overview of broken jaw assessment, emergency care, treatment options, and expected recovery.

sources.title: Natu SS et al. Epidemiology and treatment trends in jaw fractures: A retrospective study. Dental Traumatology, 2012
Zix J et al. Treatment of mandibular fractures with miniplates: ORIF vs. closed treatment. Journal of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery, 2008
Brucoli M et al. Mandibular condylar fracture management: surgery versus conservative treatment. Journal of Craniofacial Surgery, 2012
Ellis E III. Outcomes of patients with teeth in the line of mandibular angle fractures treated with miniplates. Journal of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery, 2002
Ghasemzadeh A et al. Prophylactic antibiotics in mandibular fractures: A systematic review. Oral Surgery, Oral Medicine, Oral Pathology and Oral Radiology, 2011
NICE — Head Injury: Assessment and Early Management (NG232), 2023
Champy M et al. Mandibular osteosynthesis by miniature screwed plates via a buccal approach. Journal of Maxillofacial Surgery, 1978

confusedWithConditions.name: TMJ disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TMJ disorder causes chronic jaw pain, clicking, and limited opening from internal joint derangement without bony fracture; there is no step deformity, malocclusion from acute trauma, or radiological fracture line.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dental abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A dental abscess causes localised jaw swelling and pain from infection around a tooth root; it lacks the traumatic history, malocclusion, and pathognomonic sublingual haematoma of a mandibular fracture.

confusedWithConditions.name: Osteonecrosis of the jaw
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Medication-related osteonecrosis (MRONJ) presents as exposed dead bone in the mouth, often after a dental procedure in patients on bisphosphonates or denosumab; it is a chronic condition, not an acute fracture, though it can predispose to pathological fracture.

relatedConditions.name: TMJ disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Post-traumatic TMJ disorder is a recognised sequel of condylar fractures; some patients develop chronic joint pain, clicking, and limited opening after even well-treated condylar injuries.

relatedConditions.name: Facial trauma reconstruction
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe or comminuted mandibular fractures may require secondary reconstructive surgery — bone grafting, distraction osteogenesis, or prosthetic implant placement — after initial healing.

relatedConditions.name: Orofacial pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Persistent orofacial pain after mandibular fracture, from nerve injury or post-traumatic arthritis, is a recognised complication requiring dedicated pain management.

faqs.question: How do I know if my jaw is broken?
faqs.answer: The most reliable signs of a broken jaw are that your teeth no longer fit together normally when you bite, pain that worsens sharply with jaw movement, and numbness of the lower lip or chin. Swelling, bruising under the tongue, or a step you can feel along the lower teeth strongly suggest a fracture. Any of these signs after a blow to the face warrant urgent medical assessment.

faqs.question: Can a broken jaw heal on its own without surgery?
faqs.answer: Undisplaced mandibular fractures and most condylar fractures heal well without surgery, but they still need specialist management — usually intermaxillary fixation (temporary jaw wiring) or a careful diet for 3-6 weeks. Displaced fractures of the body or angle almost always need surgical fixation with plates and screws to restore correct bite and prevent malunion.

faqs.question: How long does a broken jaw take to heal?
faqs.answer: Bone union typically occurs in 6-8 weeks for most mandibular fractures. After ORIF with miniplates, most patients resume soft foods at 4-6 weeks and normal eating at 8-12 weeks. Full jaw physiotherapy is needed to regain normal mouth opening over 8-12 weeks. Return to contact sports is usually restricted for 8-12 weeks after confirmed healing.

faqs.question: Is a jaw fracture always treated with surgery?
faqs.answer: No. About 40-50% of mandibular fractures — mainly condylar and undisplaced fractures — are managed without surgery, using temporary jaw wiring (intermaxillary fixation) and physiotherapy. Surgery with plates and screws is used for displaced body, angle, and symphysis fractures, and for dislocated condylar fractures that will not heal properly without reduction.

faqs.question: What does a broken jaw feel like?
faqs.answer: Most people describe severe pain that spikes sharply with any jaw movement — chewing, speaking, or swallowing. The lower lip and chin may feel numb or tingly. The bite feels different or uneven. Swelling and bruising develop over the lower face and under the chin, and there may be visible bruising under the tongue. Opening the mouth is painful and often limited to less than 2-3 cm.

faqs.question: Can you talk with a broken jaw?
faqs.answer: Speaking is usually painful and somewhat muffled with a broken jaw, but most people can form words because speech relies on the tongue and lips more than on jaw movement. After jaw wiring (intermaxillary fixation), speech is more restricted because the teeth are held together. Clear communication by other means may be needed in the immediate post-wiring period.

faqs.question: What can I eat with a broken jaw?
faqs.answer: During jaw wiring or the early healing phase, the diet must be fully liquid: nutritional supplement drinks, yoghurt, smoothies, liquidised soups, and pureed foods passed through the gaps between the teeth. After wiring is removed or several weeks after ORIF, a soft diet of scrambled eggs, fish, and well-cooked pasta can be added. Normal eating resumes at 8-12 weeks when the surgeon confirms healing.

faqs.question: Does a broken jaw require hospitalization?
faqs.answer: Most mandibular fractures are treated with day-surgery ORIF or as an outpatient procedure with jaw wiring. Admission for 1-3 days is typical after open surgery. Hospital stay is longer for patients with airway compromise, significant blood loss, concurrent head injury, or complex comminuted fractures requiring more extensive reconstruction.

faqs.question: Can a broken jaw cause permanent numbness?
faqs.answer: Yes, in a minority of cases. The inferior alveolar nerve passes through the mandibular canal and is frequently stretched or bruised by body and angle fractures. About 70-80% of patients recover full sensation of the lower lip and chin within 3-6 months. Fewer than 20-30% have persistent numbness beyond 12 months, which may be permanent if the nerve was severely damaged.

faqs.question: What is jaw wiring and how does it work?
faqs.answer: Jaw wiring (intermaxillary fixation) holds the upper and lower teeth together in correct alignment using arch bars wired to the teeth or small screws inserted into the jawbone connected by elastic or wire ties. This immobilises the fracture in the correct position while it heals over 3-6 weeks. You can drink through gaps between the back teeth and may use a syringe for thicker liquids.

faqs.question: How are jaw fracture plates removed?
faqs.answer: Titanium miniplates and screws used in ORIF are usually permanent and do not need removal. Removal is performed as a day-case procedure under local or general anaesthesia only if the hardware becomes infected, exposed in the mouth, or causes discomfort after healing is complete — this occurs in about 5-10% of cases.

faqs.question: Can a broken jaw affect sleep apnoea?
faqs.answer: Mandibular fractures and their surgical treatment can temporarily or permanently alter jaw position and tongue-to-palate dimensions, which may worsen or — occasionally — improve obstructive sleep apnoea. Patients with known sleep apnoea who require mandibular surgery should discuss this with their surgeon and sleep specialist, as CPAP settings may need adjustment after healing.

faqs.question: Can a broken jaw occur without a direct blow?
faqs.answer: Spontaneous or pathological fractures of the jaw can occur through abnormal bone, such as bone weakened by osteonecrosis from bisphosphonate therapy, large mandibular cysts, osteoporosis in elderly people, or tumour deposits. In these cases ordinary chewing forces can be enough to fracture the mandible. Any jaw pain or bite change in a patient with these risk factors warrants imaging.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a condylar and a body fracture?
faqs.answer: A condylar fracture involves the rounded joint head of the lower jaw at the TMJ and is usually treated without surgery in adults. A body fracture involves the tooth-bearing horizontal part of the mandible and is almost always treated with surgical plates and screws because it is displaced by chewing muscle forces and communicates with the mouth.

faqs.question: When should I go to the emergency room for a jaw injury?
faqs.answer: Go immediately to an emergency department if you have difficulty breathing or swallowing, profuse oral bleeding that will not stop, an obvious deformity or inability to close your mouth, numbness of your lower lip and chin, or any loss of consciousness after the injury. Do not eat or drink anything and keep the airway clear.

faqs.question: Can children break their jaw from a fall?
faqs.answer: Yes. Condylar fractures are the most common mandibular fracture in children, often from playground falls or bicycle accidents. They are almost universally managed without surgery in children to preserve the condyle as a growth centre. Healing is generally excellent, and the condyle has significant remodelling capacity in growing children.

faqs.question: How do miniplates in the jaw work?
faqs.answer: Titanium miniplates are small metal strips bent to fit the curved outer surface of the jawbone. They are secured with small screws through both the plate and the bone on either side of the fracture, acting as an internal splint. They allow early jaw movement while preventing the fracture from shifting during healing, and they are strong enough to withstand normal biting and chewing forces after several weeks.

faqs.question: What sports can cause a jaw fracture?
faqs.answer: Contact and collision sports — boxing, mixed martial arts, rugby, ice hockey, American football, and basketball — carry the highest risk of mandibular fracture. A properly fitted mouthguard reduces jaw fracture risk by approximately 50-60% and is strongly recommended for all participants. Cycling and equestrian sports with a risk of facial impact are also relevant.

faqs.question: Can jaw fractures affect the temporomandibular joint long term?
faqs.answer: Condylar fractures in particular can lead to post-traumatic TMJ arthritis, joint stiffness, or — rarely — ankylosis (bony fusion of the joint), causing chronic pain and restricted mouth opening. Regular physiotherapy after condylar fracture treatment is the most effective preventive measure. Severe ankylosis requires surgical release.

faqs.question: What is a pathological jaw fracture?
faqs.answer: A pathological jaw fracture breaks through bone that is already weakened by disease, not by a severe traumatic blow. Causes include osteonecrosis from bisphosphonate or denosumab treatment, mandibular cysts, osteoporosis, or jaw tumours. Diagnosis requires biopsy of the fracture margins to identify the underlying pathology, and treatment must address both the fracture and the causative condition.

metaTitle: Jaw Fracture: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: A mandibular jaw fracture causes bite misalignment, jaw pain, and lip numbness. Learn about surgery, jaw wiring, recovery time, and when to seek emergency care.
